title: Show source email content on booking request detail
description:
> *This was generated by AI during triage.*

## Agent Brief

**Category:** enhancement
**Summary:** Display the source email content on the booking request detail page so operators can read what the customer actually sent.

**Current behavior:**
The booking request detail page shows extracted fields (dates, headcount, budget, venue, status) and downstream data (drafts, event log), but never surfaces the original email. The `BookingRequest` already has a `source_inbox_message` association pointing to an `InboxMessage` record that stores `subject`, `from_name`, `from_email`, `received_at`, `body_text`, and `body_html`, but none of those fields are rendered.

**Desired behavior:**
When a booking request has a `source_inbox_message`, display the original email in a clearly labelled section on the detail page. The section should show:
- Subject line
- Sender name and email address
- Date/time received
- Email body (prefer `body_html` rendered safely; fall back to `body_text` as plain text if `body_html` is blank)

The email body must be sandboxed so arbitrary HTML from the sender cannot affect the operator UI (e.g. use an `<iframe srcdoc>` or strip/sanitize the HTML before rendering). If neither body field is present, omit the section entirely.

**Key interfaces:**
- `BookingRequest#source_inbox_message` — the `InboxMessage` association already exists; eager-load it in the controller `show` action to avoid an N+1
- `InboxMessage` attributes: `subject`, `from_name`, `from_email`, `received_at`, `body_html`, `body_text`
- The booking request detail view (`BookingRequestsController#show` + its ERB template) — add the new section after the detail grid and before the Actions section

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] The detail page shows a "Source email" section when `source_inbox_message` is present
- [ ] The section displays subject, sender (name + email), and received timestamp
- [ ] The email body is rendered (HTML preferred, plain text fallback)
- [ ] HTML email bodies cannot inject styles or scripts that affect the surrounding operator UI (sandboxed iframe or Rails `sanitize` with a safe tag allowlist)
- [ ] When `source_inbox_message` is `nil`, the section is absent and no error is raised
- [ ] The controller eager-loads `source_inbox_message` so no extra queries are fired on the show page
- [ ] Request spec or system spec covers the happy path (message present) and the nil case

**Out of scope:**
- Displaying the full email thread / all messages in the `ConversationThread`
- Inline reply composing from this section
- Attachments
- Changing how `InboxMessage` records are stored or fetched from the provider

title: Improve README with skills and make it awesome
description:
> *This was generated by AI during triage.*

## Agent Brief

**Category:** enhancement
**Summary:** Audit the README, find relevant agent skills, and rewrite it to be genuinely useful and polished.

**Current behavior:**
The README covers basic setup and project principles but is minimal — no badges, no architecture diagram, no environment variable reference, no troubleshooting section, and no contribution guide. It reads like a skeleton rather than a welcoming front door.

**Desired behavior:**
A well-structured, visually appealing README that:
- Opens with a clear one-paragraph pitch explaining what Toaster does and who it's for
- Includes status/CI badges
- Has a concise architecture overview (text or ASCII diagram showing backend ↔ frontend ↔ Solid Queue ↔ Postgres)
- Lists required environment variables with descriptions and example values
- Covers local dev setup for both backend and frontend (consolidated and accurate — remove the stale `:3001` port note if no longer needed)
- Adds a "How it works" flow (email arrives → ingested → booking request created → AI draft → human approval)
- Includes a contribution guide section (branch naming, test expectations, PR checklist)
- Has a troubleshooting FAQ for common local-dev issues (GSS/Kerberos segfault, cookie jar mismatch, queue not picking up jobs)

**Key interfaces:**
- `README.md` at repo root — rewrite in place
- Cross-check setup commands against `backend/` and `frontend/` to ensure accuracy
- Use `npx skills find readme` / `npx skills find docs` (via the `find-skills` skill) to discover any community README-generation or docs skills worth pulling in before writing

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] `npx skills find readme` (or similar query) is run and any high-quality skill is considered / adopted
- [ ] README passes a "fresh contributor" test: someone with no prior context can clone the repo and run both backend and frontend using only the README
- [ ] All environment variable references are accurate and complete
- [ ] Architecture overview section exists
- [ ] CI badge links to the GitHub Actions workflow
- [ ] Troubleshooting section covers at least the GSS/Kerberos fork-safety issue and the cookie jar mismatch

**Out of scope:**
- Adding a documentation site (e.g. Docusaurus)
- Changing any backend or frontend code

title: Move Rails app from backend/ to repo root
description:
> *This was generated by AI during triage.*

## Agent Brief

**Category:** enhancement
**Summary:** Move all Rails application files from the `backend/` subdirectory to the repository root so the project is structured like a standard Rails app.

**Current behavior:**
The repo has a `backend/` subdirectory containing the entire Rails app. This was originally laid out as a monorepo alongside a `frontend/` Next.js app. The frontend has since been removed (ADR-0004 — Rails+Hotwire monolith). The `backend/` wrapper now adds friction: Coolify must be pointed at a non-standard Dockerfile location, CI must `cd backend` before every command, and anyone cloning the repo expects `bin/rails` at the root.

**Desired behavior:**
All Rails files currently under `backend/` live at the repo root. `Gemfile`, `Dockerfile`, `docker-compose.yml`, `bin/`, `app/`, `config/`, `db/`, `spec/`, etc. are all at the top level. The repo looks and behaves like a standard Rails project.

**What to change:**

1. **Move files** — move everything inside `backend/` to the repo root. `AGENTS.md`, `CONTEXT.md`, `docs/`, and `README.md` are already at root and should stay there (merge/update as needed — e.g. `README.md` may have a backend-specific copy inside `backend/`).

2. **Update CI** — `.github/workflows/ci.yml` currently sets `working-directory: backend` and references `backend/` paths. Remove the `working-directory` override; all commands run from root.

3. **Update Coolify docker-compose** — `backend/docker-compose.yml` comment says Coolify should set base directory to `backend/`. After the move, base directory is the repo root and `docker-compose.yml` is at the root. Update the comment accordingly.

4. **Update Dockerfile** — the `Dockerfile` comment references `backend/`. Update it to reflect root placement.

5. **Update `AGENTS.md`** — remove references to `backend/` for Ruby/Rails commands (e.g. `cd backend && rvm use .` becomes `rvm use .`).

6. **Update `config/application.rb`** — the module is currently `module Backend; class Application`. Rename to `module Toaster; class Application` to match the project name. Update any references to `Backend::Application` in `config/environment.rb` and elsewhere.

7. **Search for any remaining hardcoded `backend/` path references** in configs, scripts, and docs and update them.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] No `backend/` directory exists in the repo
- [ ] `bin/rails server` and `bin/rspec` run from the repo root without `cd backend`
- [ ] `bundle exec rspec` passes from the repo root
- [ ] `bundle exec rubocop` passes
- [ ] CI (`bundle exec rspec` in `.github/workflows/ci.yml`) passes without `working-directory: backend`
- [ ] `docker build .` succeeds from the repo root
- [ ] `AGENTS.md` and `README.md` reflect root-level paths
- [ ] Module name is `Toaster::Application` (or confirm existing name is intentional and document why)

**Out of scope:**
- Any feature changes
- Reintroducing a frontend subdirectory
- Changing the database, job queue, or any application logic

title: Use local Postgres for dev/test instead of Neon
description:
> *This was generated by AI during triage.*

## Agent Brief

**Category:** enhancement
**Summary:** Remove the Neon `DATABASE_URL` from the local dev `.env` so development and test use a local Postgres socket connection, eliminating network latency from the test cycle.

**Current behavior:**
`.env` sets `DATABASE_URL` to a Neon cloud Postgres URL. `config/database.yml` propagates that URL to both `development` and `test` environments when the variable is present. Every `rspec` run makes TCP calls to Neon in AWS `us-east-1`, adding meaningful latency to each query and making tests flaky when the connection is slow or rate-limited.

**Desired behavior:**
In local development and test, Rails connects to a local Postgres instance via a Unix socket (the Rails/pg default when no `DATABASE_URL` is set). `DATABASE_URL` is only set in production and CI environments. The `.env` file ships a commented-out example `DATABASE_URL` line so developers know where to put it if they need to target Neon manually.

`config/database.yml` already has the right fallback: when `DATABASE_URL` is blank, the `test` block uses `database: backend_test<%= TEST_ENV_NUMBER %>` via local socket. No schema changes are needed.

**Key interfaces:**
- `.env` — remove or comment out the `DATABASE_URL=postgres://...neon.tech/...` line; add a comment explaining it's for production/Neon use only
- `.env.example` (create if missing) — document `DATABASE_URL` as an optional override with a placeholder value and a note that omitting it uses local Postgres
- `config/database.yml` — no changes needed; the fallback is already correct
- `AGENTS.md` / `README` — update local setup instructions if they tell developers to set `DATABASE_URL` for local dev

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] `.env` does not contain a live Neon `DATABASE_URL` (line is removed or commented out)
- [ ] `.env.example` exists and documents `DATABASE_URL` as an optional Neon override
- [ ] `bin/rails db:prepare` succeeds against local Postgres with no `DATABASE_URL` set
- [ ] `bundle exec rspec` passes against local Postgres with no `DATABASE_URL` set
- [ ] CI `DATABASE_URL` (set in `.github/workflows/ci.yml`) is unchanged — CI already uses a local Postgres service container

**Out of scope:**
- Changing the production `DATABASE_URL` (Neon remains the production database)
- Connection pooling changes (PgBouncer, etc.)
- Switching the database engine

title: Debug Coolify preview deployments
description:
> *This was generated by AI during triage.*

## Agent Brief

**Category:** enhancement
**Summary:** Investigate why Coolify preview deployments (per-PR environments) are not working and get them to a stable, usable state.

**Current behavior:**
Unknown — this issue exists to drive the investigation. Preview deployments may be failing to build, failing to start, unreachable via URL, or not being created at all when PRs are opened.

**Desired behavior:**
When a pull request is opened against `main`, Coolify automatically provisions a preview environment running the `backend/docker-compose.yml` stack (web + worker services). The preview URL is reachable and the app boots healthy (the `/up` healthcheck returns 200). When the PR is closed or merged, the preview environment is torn down.

**Investigation checklist — work through these in order:**

1. **Coolify project/application config** — Is a "Preview Deployments" application configured in Coolify pointing at this repo? What is the "Docker Compose Location" set to? Is it `backend/docker-compose.yml` with base directory `backend/`? (The compose file's comment documents the expected Coolify settings.)

2. **Build failures** — Check the Coolify build log for the most recent PR. The Dockerfile comment notes: _"No syntax line — Coolify prepends its own; two directives break the build."_ Confirm Coolify is not injecting a `# syntax=...` line that conflicts.

3. **Required env vars** — The `web` and `worker` services require `DATABASE_URL` and `RAILS_MASTER_KEY` at runtime. Preview environments need their own Postgres instance or a shared dev DB. Check whether Coolify has these env vars set for previews (either globally on the app or per-PR override). Missing `RAILS_MASTER_KEY` will cause a silent boot failure.

4. **Healthcheck timeout** — The healthcheck has a 60s `start_period` and runs `curl -f http://127.0.0.1:3000/up`. Confirm Coolify's own health polling isn't timing out before the app finishes migrating and seeding (`bin/rails db:prepare` runs on container start via the entrypoint).

5. **GitHub webhook / Coolify webhook** — Verify the Coolify webhook is registered on the GitHub repo and receiving `pull_request` events. Check Coolify's activity log for incoming webhook payloads.

6. **Networking / proxy** — Confirm the preview URL is routed through Coolify's Traefik proxy and that TLS is provisioned. If the domain is `*.preview.toaster-backend.ger3.ericdahl.dev` (or similar), ensure the wildcard DNS entry exists.

**Key interfaces:**
- `backend/docker-compose.yml` — the compose file Coolify uses; comments document expected settings
- `backend/Dockerfile` — the image build; note the no-syntax-line requirement
- `backend/config/initializers/0_pg_gssenc_fork_safety.rb` — sets `PGGSSENCMODE=disable`; must be present in the image
- `/up` endpoint — the healthcheck target (`UpController`)
- `bin/rails db:prepare` — run on startup; requires `DATABASE_URL` to point at a live Postgres

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] Opening a PR against `main` triggers a Coolify preview build within ~2 minutes
- [ ] The preview environment boots and `/up` returns 200
- [ ] The preview URL is reachable in a browser
- [ ] Closing or merging the PR tears down the preview environment
- [ ] Required env vars (`DATABASE_URL`, `RAILS_MASTER_KEY`) are documented in the Coolify app settings or this issue is updated with the chosen strategy

**Out of scope:**
- Preview-specific seed data or fixtures
- Per-PR databases (a shared dev Postgres is acceptable for previews)
- CI changes (`.github/workflows/ci.yml` runs tests; this is about the Coolify deployment layer)

title: Upgrade Rails 7.2 → 8.0
description:
> *This was generated by AI during triage.*

## Agent Brief

**Category:** enhancement
**Summary:** Bump the Rails constraint from `~> 7.2.2` to `~> 8.0`, apply the Rails 8 default configuration, and resolve any breaking changes.

**Current behavior:**
The app runs Rails 7.2 with `config.load_defaults 7.2`. The asset pipeline already uses Propshaft (the Rails 8 default), Hotwire/Turbo/Stimulus are present, and GoodJob handles background jobs — so the biggest Rails 8 defaults (Propshaft, no Solid Queue dependency) are already in place. The `net-imap` gem is explicitly declared; Rails 8 no longer bundles it, so this is already handled correctly.

**Desired behavior:**
The Gemfile pins `rails` to `~> 8.0`. `config.load_defaults` is updated to `8.0`. The test suite passes. Brakeman passes. Rubocop passes.

The agent should follow the standard Rails upgrade path:
1. Update the `rails` gem constraint and run `bundle update rails` (and any gems that need to move with it).
2. Change `config.load_defaults` from `7.2` to `8.0` and address any new defaults that conflict with existing behavior (the [Rails 8.0 upgrade guide](https://guides.rubyonrails.org/upgrading_ruby_on_rails.html) lists them).
3. Run the full test suite and fix failures.
4. Run `bundle exec brakeman -q` and resolve any new warnings.
5. Run `bundle exec rubocop` and fix any new offenses.

**Key interfaces:**
- `Gemfile` — `rails` version constraint
- `config/application.rb` — `config.load_defaults`
- Any initializers or config files that set values now controlled by Rails 8 defaults
- `GoodJob` — verify the pinned version is compatible with Rails 8 (check the GoodJob changelog/README)
- `propshaft`, `turbo-rails`, `stimulus-rails`, `importmap-rails`, `tailwindcss-rails` — verify each is compatible with Rails 8

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] `Gemfile` pins `rails` to `~> 8.0` and `bundle install` succeeds
- [ ] `config.load_defaults` is `8.0`
- [ ] `bundle exec rspec` passes with no failures
- [ ] `bundle exec brakeman -q` exits clean
- [ ] `bundle exec rubocop` exits clean
- [ ] The app boots in development (`bin/rails runner 'puts Rails.version'` prints `8.x.x`)

**Out of scope:**
- Adopting Solid Queue (GoodJob is the chosen job backend per ADR-0003)
- Adopting Solid Cache or Solid Cable
- Any feature work — this is a pure upgrade

title: Consolidate duplicated IMAP session management into Imap::Session
description:
> *This was generated by AI during triage.*

## Agent Brief

**Category:** enhancement
**Summary:** Extract the repeated IMAP connection lifecycle (open, login, yield, disconnect) from four modules into a single `Imap::Session` module, and fix the double-connection problem in the draft push path.

**Current behavior:**
The `with_imap` block — instantiate `Net::IMAP`, login, yield, and disconnect on ensure — is copied verbatim across four modules: the IMAP fetcher, the folder locator, the draft pusher, and the sent-mail reconciler. Any change to auth, SSL config, or connection behaviour requires editing all four files. Additionally, the draft pusher calls the folder locator (which opens its own TCP session to locate Drafts/Sent folders) and then opens a second TCP session for the actual IMAP append — two full connections per draft push.

**Desired behavior:**
A single `Imap::Session` module provides the IMAP lifecycle with one interface: `Imap::Session.call(imap_connection:) { |imap| ... }`. All four callers delegate to it; no local `with_imap` methods remain anywhere.

The folder locator is refactored to accept either an already-open `imap` object or a connection (for standalone use), so the draft pusher can open one session and pass the open connection into the folder locator — eliminating the double-connection per draft push.

**Key interfaces:**
- `Imap::Session` — new module; interface is `call(imap_connection:) { |imap| }` managing open/login/disconnect
- The IMAP fetcher, folder locator, draft pusher, and sent-mail reconciler all delegate lifecycle to `Imap::Session`
- The folder locator should accept an open `imap` object so callers can share a session
- The draft pusher should open exactly one IMAP session per call (folder locate + append share the connection)

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] `Imap::Session` module exists with a `call(imap_connection:) { |imap| }` interface
- [ ] The IMAP fetcher, folder locator, draft pusher, and sent-mail reconciler each have no local `with_imap` method — all delegate to `Imap::Session`
- [ ] The folder locator accepts an open `imap` object (in addition to or instead of `imap_connection:`) so callers can share a session
- [ ] The draft pusher opens only one IMAP session per call (folder location and message append use the same connection)
- [ ] All existing service specs (fetcher, folder locator, draft pusher, sent-mail reconciler) continue to pass
- [ ] `bundle exec rubocop` passes

**Out of scope:**
- OAuth / token-refresh support (future enhancement once the session seam exists)
- Connection pooling or persistent sessions across jobs
- Any changes to checkpoint semantics, ingestion orchestration, or booking request reconciliation

title: Delete SendDraftJob shallow pass-through
description:
> *This was generated by AI during triage.*

## Agent Brief

**Category:** enhancement
**Summary:** Delete the `SendDraftJob` wrapper, which is a single-line pass-through to `PushDraftJob` with no logic behind it.

**Current behavior:**
`SendDraftJob#perform` contains exactly one line: `PushDraftJob.perform_later(draft_id)`. Both callers — `DraftsController#approve` and `OpsController#retry_draft` — enqueue `SendDraftJob`, which immediately re-enqueues `PushDraftJob`. Applying the deletion test: remove `SendDraftJob` and both callers call `PushDraftJob` directly — zero complexity is lost. The job name implies a transport-abstraction seam, but there is only one transport and no logic behind the name.

**Desired behavior:**
`SendDraftJob` does not exist. `DraftsController#approve` and `OpsController#retry_draft` enqueue `PushDraftJob` directly. The job queue count (two hops per draft approval) drops to one. The approval code path is traceable in one fewer navigation hop.

If a genuine multi-transport send concept emerges later (e.g. SMTP vs. IMAP routing), it should be introduced then with real behaviour behind the seam — not preserved as an empty placeholder.

**Key interfaces:**
- `DraftsController#approve` — should enqueue `PushDraftJob` directly
- `OpsController#retry_draft` — should enqueue `PushDraftJob` directly
- `SendDraftJob` class and its spec file should be deleted entirely

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] `SendDraftJob` class is deleted
- [ ] `SendDraftJob` spec file is deleted
- [ ] `DraftsController#approve` enqueues `PushDraftJob` directly
- [ ] `OpsController#retry_draft` enqueues `PushDraftJob` directly
- [ ] No remaining references to `SendDraftJob` anywhere in the codebase (routes, configs, specs, jobs)
- [ ] All existing controller and request specs continue to pass
- [ ] `bundle exec rubocop` passes

**Out of scope:**
- Any changes to `PushDraftJob`, `ReconcileDraftJob`, or the draft reconciliation flow
- Introducing a real multi-transport abstraction (that is a separate future decision)

title: Extract BookingRequests::FieldExtractor from Extract service
description:
> *This was generated by AI during triage.*

## Agent Brief

**Category:** enhancement
**Summary:** Extract the pure text-to-structured-fields logic from `BookingRequests::Extract` into a standalone `BookingRequests::FieldExtractor` module so it can be unit-tested without a database.

**Current behavior:**
`BookingRequests::Extract` does five distinct things inside one transaction: contact upsert, thread upsert, regex field extraction (event date, headcount, budget), booking request upsert, and canonical message upsert. The pure field extraction logic — regex scanning, ambiguity detection, missing-field tracking, and status derivation — has zero DB dependencies but is buried alongside five DB writes. Any test of regex correctness must factory-create an `InboxMessage` and hit the database.

**Desired behavior:**
A standalone module `BookingRequests::FieldExtractor` accepts `subject:` and `body_text:` keyword arguments and returns an extraction hash: `{ event_date:, headcount:, budget_cents:, missing_fields:, review_reasons:, snapshot:, status: }`. All regex patterns (month-name date, headcount, budget) and the `single_value` ambiguity logic live in `FieldExtractor`.

`BookingRequests::Extract` becomes a thin coordinator: it calls `FieldExtractor` with the inbox message's subject and body, then handles all DB writes exactly as today. Its transaction and persistence behaviour are unchanged.

The seam between FieldExtractor and Extract is the extraction result hash — not the raw InboxMessage — so a future LLM-based extraction path (the `AiRun` model already exists) can be wired in by composing or swapping the extractor behind the same interface.

**Key interfaces:**
- `BookingRequests::FieldExtractor` — new module; interface is `call(subject:, body_text:)` returning the extraction hash described above
- `BookingRequests::Extract` — delegates to `FieldExtractor`; its `Result` struct, transaction, and DB writes are unchanged
- Unit specs for `FieldExtractor` should use plain strings with no FactoryBot or DB access

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] `BookingRequests::FieldExtractor` exists with a `call(subject:, body_text:)` interface returning the extraction hash
- [ ] All regex constants and `single_value` ambiguity logic live in `FieldExtractor`, not in `Extract`
- [ ] `BookingRequests::Extract` delegates field extraction to `FieldExtractor` and is otherwise unchanged
- [ ] `FieldExtractor` unit specs use only plain strings — no FactoryBot, no DB
- [ ] All existing reconcile and extract specs continue to pass
- [ ] `bundle exec rubocop` passes

**Out of scope:**
- Changing the regex patterns or extraction logic itself
- Wiring in an LLM-based extractor
- Any changes to the reconcile or post-ingestion orchestration

title: Consolidate duplicated IMAP session management into Imap::Session
description:
## What to build

The with_imap block — open Net::IMAP connection, login, yield, disconnect on ensure — is copied verbatim across four modules: Imap::Fetcher, Imap::FolderLocator, Drafts::ImapDraftPusher, and Drafts::SentMailReconciler. Any change to auth, SSL config, OAuth token refresh, or connection reuse requires editing all four files. Additionally, ImapDraftPusher calls FolderLocator (which opens its own TCP session) then opens a second session for the append — two TCP connections per draft push.

Introduce Imap::Session with a single interface: Imap::Session.call(imap_connection:) { |imap| ... } that manages open, login, and disconnect. Refactor all four callers to delegate lifecycle to it. Refactor FolderLocator to accept an already-open imap object so the folder-locate + draft-push path in ImapDraftPusher can share one session instead of opening two.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Imap::Session module exists with a single call(imap_connection:) { |imap| } interface
- [ ] Imap::Fetcher, Imap::FolderLocator, Drafts::ImapDraftPusher, and Drafts::SentMailReconciler all delegate IMAP lifecycle to Imap::Session — no local with_imap methods remain
- [ ] Imap::FolderLocator accepts an open imap object (and/or imap_connection: for standalone use) so callers can share a session
- [ ] ImapDraftPusher opens only one IMAP session per draft push (folder locate + append share the same connection)
- [ ] All existing service and job specs continue to pass
- [ ] rubocop passes

## Blocked by

None - can start immediately

The pure text-to-structured-fields logic (event date, headcount, budget regex scanning, ambiguity detection, status derivation) has zero DB dependencies but is buried alongside five DB writes. Any test of regex correctness must factory-create an InboxMessage and hit the database.

Extract the pure field extraction logic into a standalone module — BookingRequests::FieldExtractor — that accepts subject: and body_text: keyword arguments and returns the extraction hash (event_date, headcount, budget_cents, missing_fields, review_reasons, snapshot, status). BookingRequests::Extract becomes a thin coordinator that calls FieldExtractor then handles DB writes.

The seam between FieldExtractor and Extract is the extraction result hash — not the raw InboxMessage. Future AI-based extraction (an LLM path, suggested by the existing AiRun model) can be wired in behind the same seam by composing or swapping the extractor.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] BookingRequests::FieldExtractor exists as a standalone module/class taking subject: and body_text: and returning the extraction hash
- [ ] All regex patterns and single_value ambiguity logic live in FieldExtractor, not in Extract
- [ ] BookingRequests::Extract delegates to FieldExtractor; its transaction and DB writes are unchanged
- [ ] Unit specs for FieldExtractor use plain strings — no FactoryBot, no DB
- [ ] Existing reconcile and extract specs continue to pass
- [ ] rubocop passes

None - can start immediately

title: feat: subject keyword filters on inbox connection to route to venues
description:
## Context
An operator has one IMAP inbox receiving inquiries for multiple venues. Subject lines contain venue-identifying keywords. We need to map those keywords to the right venue when creating a `BookingRequest`.

## Desired behaviour
- When creating/editing an IMAP connection, the operator can add keyword → venue pairs
- On ingest, the first keyword found (case-insensitive) in the subject sets the venue on the `BookingRequest`
- No match = no venue assigned (existing behaviour)

## Data model
```
inbox_filters
  id
  imap_connection_id   fk → imap_connections
  venue_id             fk → venues
  keyword              string, not null
  priority             integer, not null, default: 0
  created_at / updated_at
```

## Scope
- [ ] Migration: create `inbox_filters` table
- [ ] `InboxFilter` model with validations
- [ ] `InboxIngestion::FilterMatcher` service: matches subject against connection's filters by priority, returns `venue_id` or nil
- [ ] Wire into `InboxIngestion::Sync`
- [ ] Rails ERB UI: add/remove keyword → venue rows on the mail connection form (Hotwire/Turbo Frames)
- [ ] Request + service specs

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Operator can manage keyword → venue pairs from the mail connections UI
- [ ] Ingest applies the first matching filter by priority to set `booking_request.venue`
- [ ] No match leaves venue nil
- [ ] `bundle exec parallel_rspec spec/` passes
- [ ] TDD: spec first

title: Operator inbox: thread grouping and full thread view
description:
## Problem

Ops inbox lists flat inbound messages. Operators need **one row per mail thread**, and selecting a thread should show the **full conversation**: inbound and outbound `InboxMessage` rows, plus **all `Draft` lifecycle** (pending, approved, sent; **rejected** shown **collapsed**). Keep the **Request snapshot** panel on the side.

## Alignment (CONTEXT.md, ADR 0001, ADR 0002)

- **CONTEXT.md — inbox language:** The transcript is built from **inbox messages** (one row per provider message id per account) and **drafts** tied to **booking requests**; grouping uses persisted **`provider_thread_id`** from ingestion, not a new thread entity.
- **ADR 0001 (reconcile):** This work is **read/UI + ops API** for existing rows. It does **not** change **`BookingRequests::PostIngestion` / `Reconcile`** vs **`Transition`**; ingestion continues to reconcile after upsert as today.
- **ADR 0002 (multi-venue / lineage):** Does **not** implement **connection lineage**, **venue routing**, or dedupe strategy changes. **Implication:** thread grouping uses **`account_id` + `provider` + `provider_thread_id`** (singleton: same **`provider`** + anchor **`inbox_message.id`**) so thread ids from different providers—or later, different connections with the same provider—do not merge incorrectly. Further key changes may follow once **connection lineage** on `InboxMessage` lands.
- **Request snapshot** may show **`venue_id`** when set; CONTEXT/0002 note that routing into **`venue_id`** is still a separate pipeline—this issue must not imply venue is inferred from the transcript alone.

## Out of scope (follow-ups)

- **Draft vs ingested outbound diff** and training pipelines from that diff.
- **Heuristic merge/dedup** of sent draft + outbound send (v1 shows **both** with clear labels).
- **Pagination** beyond a fixed thread cap (see acceptance).
- **ADR 0002** connection linkage, routing rules, and dedupe policy (track separately; may require revisiting thread key).

## Design decisions (agreed)

- **Thread key:** `account_id` + **`provider`** + `provider_thread_id`. **Singleton** when `provider_thread_id` is null: one thread per message (anchor on that `inbox_message.id`, same `provider`).
- **Thread list sort:** `max` of inbound `received_at` and draft activity timestamps (see ordering).
- **List cap:** ~**50 threads** in v1 (no cursor pagination).
- **API:** New `Ops::InboxThreadsController` — `#index` thread list, `#show` full payload (timeline + primary booking snapshot). Prefer deprecating flat `inbox_messages#index` after UI cutover.
- **Booking resolution:** **Primary** booking when multiple share the thread (unlikely): **newest non-terminal** else newest `created_at`; **banner** if count > 1.
- **Transcript ordering:** Inbound/outbound messages by `received_at` (fallback `created_at`). Drafts: `created_at` until `sent`, then **`sent_at`**. Add **`drafts.sent_at`**; backfill existing `sent` rows with **`sent_at = updated_at`**. **`SendDraftJob`** must set **`sent_at`** when marking sent.
- **URL:** v1 **deep link** — `account_id` + **`provider`** + thread discriminator (`provider_thread_id` or singleton message id); **auto-select first thread** when no params (current behavior).
- **Rejected drafts:** visible but **collapsed** by default.

## Development seeds (required for this issue)

Update **`db/seeds/development_demo.rb`** (still idempotent via `find_or_initialize_by`) so `bin/rails db:seed` exercises the new UI without manual data:

1. **Outbound mail:** Add a helper (e.g. `upsert_outbound` or extend `upsert_inbox` with `direction:`) to create **`InboxMessage` with `direction: outbound`** on at least one existing **`provider_thread_id`**, with realistic `body_text`, `received_at` / `created_at` ordering after an inbound message. Prefer the **`seed:thread:multi`** thread or a dedicated **`seed:thread:outbound-demo`** thread tied to a booking so the Request snapshot still appears.
2. **Draft lifecycle:** Beyond today’s **`pending_review`** only, seed on one booking (can be the same outbound-demo thread):
   - one **`sent`** draft with **`sent_at`** set (and body text that clearly differs from the outbound body so v1 “two labeled bubbles” is obvious);
   - one **`rejected`** draft (body + status) to verify **collapsed-by-default** behavior;
   - optional: one **`approved`** not yet sent if useful for status labels.
3. **Singleton thread (optional but quick):** One inbound message with **`provider_thread_id: nil`** to verify **singleton** grouping (`account_id` + **`provider`** + message anchor), unless that complicates `upsert_inbox` uniqueness—if so, document skip in a one-line comment in the seeder.

Keep **`provider: "seed"`** and existing idempotency keys so re-running seeds does not duplicate rows.
acceptance_criteria: Given ops inbox, the left column lists threads (not individual inbound messages), capped at ~50, sorted by last activity across messages and drafts. Thread identity includes account_id, provider, and provider_thread_id (or singleton message id). Given a selected thread, the center shows a chronological transcript of inbound and outbound InboxMessage rows and all Draft rows (rejected collapsed by default). Given a thread, the right panel shows the existing Request snapshot for the primary booking, with a warning if multiple bookings share the thread. Given /inbox with query params for account, provider, and thread, the same thread is selected after refresh. Migration adds drafts.sent_at; existing sent drafts backfill sent_at from updated_at; SendDraftJob sets sent_at on send. Request specs cover InboxThreadsController; frontend tests updated for thread list + transcript. After bin/rails db:seed in development, at least one thread includes outbound InboxMessage seed data plus sent and rejected drafts (with sent_at on sent) so operators can verify the full transcript locally without fixtures-only coverage.

title: Wire SendDraftJob to outbound email provider
description:
## Context

The IMAP send pipeline is almost entirely built:

- `PushDraftJob` pushes the draft body to the IMAP Drafts folder and records `imap_draft_uid` + `original_body` on the draft
- `SentMailReconciler` searches the IMAP Sent folder for a matching message, computes body similarity, and classifies the outcome (`approved` / `modified` / `rejected`), updating `draft.status` and `draft.sent_at`
- `SendDraftJob` delegates to `PushDraftJob`

**What's missing:**

1. Nothing calls `SentMailReconciler` — no scheduled job polls for sent drafts
2. No outbound `Message` record is created when the reconciler detects a send
3. `BookingRequest` status does not update after a draft is sent

## Scope

### 1. Reconciler polling job
Create `ReconcileSentDraftsJob` that:
- Finds all `Draft` records with `imap_draft_uid` present and status `pending_review`
- For each, calls `SentMailReconciler` with the account's active IMAP connection
- Skips gracefully if no active IMAP connection exists

Schedule it on a short interval (e.g. every 2 minutes) via `InboxSyncScheduler` or GoodJob cron.

### 2. Outbound Message record
After `SentMailReconciler` detects a send (outcome `approved` or `modified`), create an outbound `Message` record:
- `direction: :outbound`
- `account`, `conversation_thread`, `booking_request` from the draft
- `body_text` from the sent body
- `sent_at` from the reconciler result

### 3. BookingRequest state update
After a draft is confirmed sent, transition `BookingRequest` to `confirmed` if currently in `reviewing`.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `ReconcileSentDraftsJob` exists and is scheduled
- [ ] Job calls `SentMailReconciler` for each pending IMAP draft
- [ ] Outbound `Message` record created on detected send
- [ ] `BookingRequest` transitions to `confirmed` after send
- [ ] Job specs cover: send detected (approved), send detected (modified), no sent match yet (pending), no IMAP connection
- [ ] `bundle exec parallel_rspec spec/` passes
- [ ] TDD: spec first

title: Code Review: Ensure Self-Documenting Code and Strategic Commenting
description:
Perform an in-depth review of the codebase to ensure adherence to the following principles:

- **Prefer self-documenting code:** Prioritize clear naming conventions and code structure, making the code as understandable as possible without supplemental commentary.
- **Use comments strategically:** Add comments only to clarify non-obvious rationale, such as domain-specific rules, safety or correctness invariants, or if there are noteworthy security or performance tradeoffs. Avoid comments that simply narrate straightforward lines of code.

The goal is to improve clarity, maintainability, and align with best practices throughout the project.

title: [Architecture] Vitest coverage for backend proxy and server Rails fetch
description:
## Summary

Add focused Vitest coverage for the **Next.js → Rails** proxy and server fetch helpers so cookie forwarding and method pass-through do not regress silently.

## Context

The generic backend proxy (`/api/backend/[...]`) and `serverFetchBackend` are critical for session cookies. Coverage today is thin; failures appear in manual or integrated runs only.

## Problem

- Wrong base URL or broken proxy behaviour causes hard-to-debug 401s after login.
- No fast feedback loop for contributors changing env or fetch helpers.

## Proposed direction

- Tests that exercise the route handler with mocked `fetch` to upstream or synthetic `Request`/`Response` (project-appropriate minimal harness).
- Cover at least: GET forward, cookie header forward, and one error path.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Vitest tests committed under `frontend/tests/` (or agreed location) for the proxy route and/or `serverFetchBackend`.
- [ ] CI runs them as part of existing `yarn test` (or documented script addition).
- [ ] Comment in test file pointing to project rule: credential-bearing browser traffic and same-origin proxy.

## Related files

`frontend/src/app/api/backend/[[...path]]/route.ts`, `frontend/src/lib/server-toaster-session.ts`, `frontend/src/lib/toaster-fetch.ts`

title: [Architecture] Introduce scheduler seam for inbox sync jobs
description:
## Summary

Hide concrete sync job classes behind a small **scheduling** module so HTTP layers do not hard-code `SyncImapJob` / `SyncAgentMailboxJob` (and future providers).

## Context

Controllers `perform_later` on specific job classes. That couples routes to queue topology and provider-specific job names.

## Problem

- Adding a provider or changing how sync is scheduled (fan-out, queue name, deduping) touches many controllers.
- Tests must stub multiple job classes for the same conceptual action: “run **inbox ingestion** for this connection.”

## Proposed direction

- One module whose **interface** is “schedule ingestion for connection X” (or account-level variant), implemented by enqueueing the right job internally.
- Controllers and request specs depend only on that seam.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] New indirection in place; controllers call the scheduler, not raw job constants (except inside the scheduler implementation).
- [ ] Request specs updated to expect the scheduler or assert enqueue via test adapter without naming concrete job classes at the controller level—unless a provider truly requires an exception (documented).
- [ ] Behaviour unchanged: same jobs enqueued with equivalent arguments for each trigger today.

## Related files

`backend/app/controllers/imap/`, `backend/app/controllers/agent_mailbox/`, `backend/app/jobs/sync_imap_job.rb`, `backend/app/jobs/sync_agent_mailbox_job.rb`

title: [Architecture] Decouple ops HTTP from Solid Queue, drafts, and heavy includes
description:
## Summary

Decouple **ops** HTTP from Solid Queue internals, draft jobs, and heavy eager-loading so the ops API has a maintainable query/presentation layer.

## Context

`OpsController` and ops inbox controllers touch `SolidQueue::*`, enqueue `SendDraftJob`, and shape nested inbox + booking JSON. Observability and mail domains are intertwined at the controller layer.

## Problem

- Changes to queue schema or draft sending force edits at HTTP handlers.
- Large `includes` and ad hoc JSON build-up make request specs brittle and obscure authorization vs presentation.

## Proposed direction

- Introduce service or presenter objects for “ops dashboard aggregates,” “ops inbox message JSON,” and “retry draft” so controllers stay thin.
- Keep Mission Control / queue read models behind one module per concern.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Ops controllers delegate non-trivial reads and payload shaping to dedicated modules (names match domain language from `CONTEXT.md` where possible).
- [ ] Request specs still cover auth and main JSON contracts; refactor existing examples rather than dropping coverage.
- [ ] No new user-facing behaviour required—internal structure + parity.

## Related files

`backend/app/controllers/ops_controller.rb`, `backend/app/controllers/ops/inbox_messages_controller.rb`, `backend/app/jobs/send_draft_job.rb`, `backend/config/initializers/mission_control.rb`

title: [Architecture] Wire or explicitly defer post-ingestion booking (Reconcile/Transition)
description:
## Summary

Clarify and implement the **booking** lifecycle **after** inbox messages are persisted: wire `BookingRequests::Reconcile` / `BookingRequests::Transition` into real runtime paths or explicitly defer.

## Context

`CONTEXT.md` describes **inbox ingestion** (fetch + upsert + checkpoint). Services `BookingRequests::Reconcile` and `BookingRequests::Transition` exist and have specs but **no** references under `backend/app/` outside tests—so the “message landed → booking work” pipeline is not a single visible seam in production code.

## Problem

- Product and ops assumptions about booking extraction may not match what actually runs after sync.
- Bugs hide in wiring, not in well-tested pure services.

## Proposed direction

Pick one explicit outcome and record it:

1. **Integrate:** Post-ingestion (job or callback) calls into a single orchestration module that uses Reconcile/Transition as appropriate; add integration or job specs.
2. **Defer:** If not shipping soon, mark services as experimental, reduce confusion in ops UI, and file follow-ups.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Written decision (issue comment or short ADR): integrated vs deferred, with owner.
- [ ] If integrated: at least one app-level caller (job or service) from the ingestion/booking path; tests prove the call chain.
- [ ] If deferred: docs or code comments so future work does not assume these run in prod.

## Related files

`backend/app/services/inbox_ingestion/sync.rb`, `backend/app/services/booking_requests/reconcile.rb`, `backend/app/services/booking_requests/transition.rb`, relevant jobs under `backend/app/jobs/`
close_reason: Wire PostIngestion→Reconcile from InboxIngestion::Sync; ADR0001; Transition stays manual-only

title: [Architecture] Deduplicate IMAP vs AgentMailbox connection/sync controllers
description:
## Summary

Reduce duplication between **IMAP** and **AgentMailbox** connection/sync controllers while preserving provider-specific params and models.

## Context

Rails routes expose parallel CRUD and “enqueue sync” flows for IMAP and AgentMailbox. Controllers are largely copy-paste with different model classes and param keys.

## Problem

- Authorization, job enqueue, and JSON shape rules can drift between the two stacks.
- Twice the request specs and fixes for the same behavioural concern.

## Proposed direction

- Extract shared behaviour (forbidden cross-account, enqueue pattern, response structure) behind a small internal API or concern, with provider-specific hooks for params and job class if still needed.
- Prefer one request-spec matrix that covers both providers where behaviour matches.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Shared code path for “create connection + optionally enqueue” and “enqueue sync for connection” with provider-specific inputs isolated.
- [ ] No behaviour regression: existing request specs pass; add missing parity if IMAP lacked a spec AgentMailbox had (or document intentional differences).
- [ ] Brief comment or doc on what must stay provider-specific (checkpoint semantics stay in ingestion adapters per `CONTEXT.md`).

## Related files

`backend/app/controllers/imap/`, `backend/app/controllers/agent_mailbox/`, `backend/config/routes.rb`

title: [Architecture] Unify operator inbox network path and view-model mapping
description:
## Summary

Make the operator inbox use **one** network and parsing story for inbox messages so RSC and client components do not drift.

## Context

The inbox UI mixes server-side fetch toward Rails, client `toasterFetch`, and ops API routes. JSON-to-view-model mapping is duplicated between the page and `operator-inbox-client`.

## Problem

- Same feature, multiple transport paths—hard to reason about and easy to misconfigure env (localhost vs proxy).
- Duplicate parsing means schema tweaks require two edits and risk silent UI bugs.

## Proposed direction

- Single source for “operator inbox row” mapping (types + normalization).
- Align on one browser/server strategy (e.g. always credential-bearing traffic via `/api/backend` where applicable).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Inbox message / booking-related view shapes produced in one module consumed by both server and client paths.
- [ ] Documented rule for which URL strategy the inbox uses in dev and prod.
- [ ] Regression coverage for the mapper (Vitest) with fixture JSON.

## Related files

`frontend/src/app/inbox/page.tsx`, `frontend/src/components/inbox/operator-inbox-client.tsx`, `frontend/src/app/api/ops/inbox_messages/**/*.ts`

title: [Architecture] Consolidate Next.js → Rails fetch and session seam
description:
## Summary

Consolidate how the Next.js app talks to Rails so cookie/session behaviour, base URLs, and error handling are not repeated at every call site.

## Context

Today the frontend splits **server** vs **browser** fetch paths (`serverRailsBaseUrl` / proxy vs `browserToasterApiBase`), plus separate helpers (`server-toaster-session`, `toaster-fetch`, `toaster-api`). Ops routes duplicate session checks. Wrong combination breaks auth cookies (see project notes on same-origin `/api/backend` proxy).

## Problem

- Callers must understand env, cookies, and URL strategy together—a **shallow** spread of HTTP details.
- Regressions tend to show up only in manual testing or production-like runs.

## Proposed direction

- One obvious entry point (or a tiny family of modules) for “authenticated Rails JSON” from RSC vs client, with shared typing and error parsing where it helps.
- Document the invariant: when browser code must use credentials, which base URL path is required.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] New code paths for account/session APIs go through the consolidated helper(s); legacy call sites migrated or explicitly grandfathered with a comment.
- [ ] Short developer note (README fragment or `frontend/AGENTS.md` section) describing server vs browser and the proxy rule.
- [ ] Unit tests for the shared helper(s) (happy path + one failure shape), not necessarily every page.

## Related files

`frontend/src/lib/toaster-api.ts`, `frontend/src/lib/toaster-fetch.ts`, `frontend/src/lib/server-toaster-session.ts`, `frontend/src/app/api/backend/[[...path]]/route.ts`

title: Add first-party email/password session auth for app users
description:
## Problem
User-facing app routes currently rely on environment-driven account selection and do not enforce per-user authentication/session access. This blocks safe multi-user usage and leaves account-scoped endpoints accessible without a logged-in user context.

## Goal
Implement first-party email/password auth using Rails-backed sessions and cookie auth for frontend + backend user flows.

## Scope
- Add secure password auth to `User` (`password_digest`, `has_secure_password`, email normalization).
- Add auth session endpoints:
  - `POST /auth/login`
  - `POST /auth/logout`
  - `GET /auth/me`
- Add backend current-user/session plumbing and `require_authenticated_user!` guard for app-facing APIs.
- Enforce account scoping from `current_user.account` in user-facing account APIs.
- Add frontend login/logout UX and session bootstrap/redirect behavior for protected routes.
- Replace env-based `TOASTER_ACCOUNT_ID` reliance on protected user pages with session-derived account context.

## Out of scope (for this issue)
- Replacing existing `/ops/*` token auth with user auth.
- OAuth/social login.

## Backend tasks
- [ ] Add request specs (TDD first) for login/logout/me and protected endpoint authorization behavior.
- [ ] Add migration for `users.password_digest` (+ needed constraints/indexes).
- [ ] Update `User` model with `has_secure_password` and password validations.
- [ ] Implement auth sessions controller/routes.
- [ ] Add `current_user` + `require_authenticated_user!` in base controller/concern.
- [ ] Refactor account-scoped controllers to use authenticated account context.

## Frontend tasks
- [ ] Add login page/form for email/password.
- [ ] Add logout action.
- [ ] Add session bootstrap for protected pages (`/email-accounts`, `/inbox`) and redirect unauthenticated users to login.
- [ ] Read account context from `/auth/me` response instead of env-only account id.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Unauthenticated requests to protected user APIs return `401` JSON.
- [ ] Authenticated users can only access data for their own account.
- [ ] Login creates session cookie; logout clears session.
- [ ] Protected frontend pages require active session.
- [ ] `backend` auth specs pass and `frontend` `yarn build` passes.

## Notes
Prefer incremental rollout with minimal behavior changes beyond auth gates; keep ops token flow unchanged initially.
close_reason: Shipped session auth: bcrypt, routes, guarded account APIs, CORS credentials, Next proxy + login + protected pages + ops inbox proxy.

title: Deprecate and remove Gmail-specific backend flow (controllers/routes/services)
description:
## Context
We still have Gmail-specific backend endpoints and supporting domain code (OAuth, connection management, watch renewal, webhook processing). Current product direction appears focused on IMAP/AgentMail.

## Problem
Gmail-specific code paths increase maintenance surface and ops complexity if they are no longer part of the intended product behavior.

## Goal
Safely deprecate and remove Gmail-specific backend flow, or explicitly confirm and keep it. This issue tracks the removal path.

## Scope
- Audit and remove Gmail routes/controllers if deprecated:
  - `/gmail/oauth/start`
  - `/gmail/oauth/callback`
  - `/accounts/:account_id/gmail/connections` (+ reconnect/resync)
- Remove or migrate supporting services/domain pieces:
  - `GmailOauthService`
  - `GmailWatchService`
  - `GmailConnection` model usage
  - Gmail webhook job/event flow where no longer needed
- Clean up ops endpoints/metrics referencing Gmail connection state
- Remove/update related request/service/model/job specs
- Update docs/runbooks to reflect supported inbox providers

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Gmail-specific routes are removed (or intentionally retained with rationale)
- [ ] No dead Gmail services/jobs/models remain in active code paths
- [ ] Ops endpoints no longer report obsolete Gmail metrics
- [ ] Test suite passes with updated coverage
- [ ] Documentation reflects only supported provider flows

## Safety / Rollout Notes
- Confirm no active customers/tenants still depend on Gmail OAuth before removal.
- If needed, use a feature flag/deprecation window before hard delete.
- Prefer incremental PRs (route/controller removal, then domain cleanup, then docs).
close_reason: Completed via incremental issues toaster-8dt, toaster-jmd, and toaster-3w6 covering route removal, domain cleanup, and ops/data cleanup.

title: Extract shared ops token auth into controller concern
description:

## Scope

- [ ] All ops controllers use the shared concern
- [ ] Existing ops auth behavior unchanged
- [ ] Request specs pass
- [ ] `bundle exec rubocop` / `standardrb` clean on touched files

title: Add Mission Control UI for Solid Queue operations
description:
## Problem
We are using Solid Queue, but we do not currently have an internal admin UI to inspect and operate background jobs (queues, retries, failures, and throughput) in one place.

## Proposal
Integrate Mission Control for Solid Queue so operators can:
- view queue/job state in production and staging
- inspect failed/retried jobs
- retry/discard jobs from a controlled admin surface

## Scope
- Add and configure the Mission Control gem/package for Rails + Solid Queue
- Mount the Mission Control route(s) in `routes.rb`
- Restrict access to ops/admin users only (authn/authz)
- Ensure environment-safe behavior (enabled in staging/prod, optional in dev)
- Add docs for setup and usage

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Mission Control is available at a documented route
- [ ] Access is restricted to authorized users
- [ ] Queue state, failures, and retries are visible
- [ ] Retry/discard operations are functional
- [ ] Request/system tests cover access control and route availability
- [ ] README or ops docs include runbook notes

## Notes
- Prefer least-privilege access and explicit environment gating.
- Include any migration/deploy steps required for rollout.
close_reason: Mission Control at /jobs with HTTP Basic outside dev/test; README and AGENTS documented; request specs for auth. Dev/test no auth by design.

Create interface for users to add an email account
description:
Develop a user interface that allows users to add a new email account to the application. The interface should provide clear form fields for email address, password, any necessary server settings (such as IMAP/SMTP server, port, security protocol), and a submit/save button. Include validation to ensure the information provided is correct and handle error cases (such as failed authentication or incomplete details).

**Requirements:**
- Design a simple, user-friendly form for account entry.
- Ensure support for common email providers (e.g., Gmail, Outlook, Yahoo) and allow manual server entry.
- Provide feedback to users on successful or failed addition.
- Validate email address format and required fields.
- Ensure secure handling of credentials.

**Outcome:**
A functional screen/component in the app enabling users to successfully connect their email accounts.

title: Standard IMAP inbox support alongside Gmail
description:
## Summary
Add support for standard IMAP inboxes so Toaster can ingest email from providers that are not Gmail.

## Scope
- Introduce a provider-agnostic inbound mailbox abstraction instead of assuming Gmail everywhere.
- Add account-scoped IMAP connection configuration for common IMAP hosts.
- Support polling-based sync for IMAP inboxes.
- Persist inbound messages using the same canonical message and booking-request workflow used by other providers.
- Normalize provider identifiers so IMAP-backed messages can coexist with Gmail and agent-mailbox sources.
- Preserve room for provider-specific capabilities without making IMAP a second-class path.

## Requirements
- Gmail cannot remain the only real inbox integration path.
- IMAP support should work with standard providers, not just one vendor.
- Ingestion must stay idempotent and auditable.
- Provider-specific auth and sync details should live behind a clean interface.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Toaster can store and use IMAP connection settings for an account.
- [ ] A polling sync path can fetch new IMAP messages and persist them safely.
- [ ] IMAP messages are normalized into the existing inbox and booking workflow without special-case hacks.
- [ ] Duplicate fetches do not create duplicate records.
- [ ] Provider abstractions are clear enough to support Gmail, IMAP, and future sources side by side.
- [ ] Tests cover IMAP connection setup, polling sync, and dedupe behavior.
- [ ] Implementation follows TDD.

## Outcome
Toaster can ingest booking emails from standard IMAP providers instead of being boxed into Gmail-only assumptions.

title: Add operational guardrails: Solid Queue, Mission Control, observability, and recovery paths

title: Operator UI for requests, threads, tasks, and drafts
description:
## Context

The Rails+Hotwire monolith migration (Phase 2) built the scaffolding and core views. The following operator surfaces already exist:

**Done:**
- `GET /booking_requests` — list with status badges
- `GET /booking_requests/:id` — detail with draft body + approve/reject buttons
- `POST /booking_requests/:id/drafts/:id/approve` — approves, enqueues SendDraftJob
- `POST /booking_requests/:id/drafts/:id/reject`
- `GET /mail_connections`, `GET /mail_connections/new`, `POST /mail_connections`

**Still missing (scope of this issue):**

### 1. Booking request state transitions
Operator needs buttons to manually move a request through its lifecycle:
- `pending → reviewing` (start working it)
- `reviewing → confirmed` or `reviewing → rejected` or `reviewing → cancelled`
- `POST /booking_requests/:id/transition` action on `BookingRequestsController`

### 2. Event log on booking request detail
- Show `EventLog` entries (what happened, when) on `show.html.erb`
- Read-only, chronological

### 3. Inbox thread view (operator-facing)
The `/ops/inbox_threads` endpoint exists but is ops-token-gated JSON. Replace/supplement with an auth-session-gated HTML view:
- `GET /inbox_threads` — list of threads with last message preview
- `GET /inbox_threads/:id` — thread detail: messages in order, linked booking request

### 4. Nav / shell
- Persistent nav bar across all HTML pages: Booking Requests | Mail Connections | Inbox Threads | logout
- Currently each page is isolated with no nav

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Operator can transition a booking request status from the detail page
- [ ] Event log renders on the booking request detail page
- [ ] `GET /inbox_threads` lists threads (auth-gated, account-scoped)
- [ ] `GET /inbox_threads/:id` shows messages + linked booking request
- [ ] Nav bar present on all pages
- [ ] Request specs cover state transitions, cross-account 404, and unauthenticated redirect
- [ ] `bundle exec parallel_rspec spec/` passes

## Implementation notes
- All HTML views use ERB + Tailwind (same pattern as existing booking_requests views)
- Account scoping via `current_user.account` — no ops token needed
- TDD: spec first

title: Outbound reply send pipeline and draft approval workflow
description:
## Summary
Complete the outbound reply workflow: implement actual sending via AgentMail for AgentMail-backed flows, and expose the draft approval UI.

## What already exists
- `Draft` model with `pending_review`, `approved`, `rejected`, `sent` states
- `SendDraftJob` stub (marks draft sent but does not call any send API)
- `Message` model for persisting sent messages

## Remaining scope
- Implement AgentMail API send in `SendDraftJob` for AgentMail-connected accounts
- Route outbound sending through the correct backend for the active mailbox/provider setup
- Persist outbound `Message` rows after successful send
- Update thread and booking-request state after send
- Expose approve, edit, discard, and send actions via the ops API
- Surface draft review in the operator UI (see issue #9)

## Requirements
- Provider-aware: sending must use the correct outbound path for the mailbox in use
- AgentMail is the active outbound path for AgentMail/testing/transactional flows
- Manual approval is first-class in V1
- Send must be retry-safe and auditable

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `SendDraftJob` sends via AgentMail API for AgentMail-backed accounts
- [ ] Outbound sends create `Message` rows
- [ ] Thread and booking-request state update correctly after send
- [ ] Failed sends are recoverable and visible in Mission Control
- [ ] Request and job specs cover send success, send failure, and provider routing
- [ ] Implementation follows TDD

## Outcome
Toaster gets a controlled outbound workflow with approval gates and recoverable send behavior.

title: LLM-backed extraction and AI decisioning pipeline
description:
## Summary
Replace regex extraction with an LLM-backed pipeline for structured field extraction and next-step decisioning on inbound messages from the provider-agnostic inbox pipeline (IMAP and AgentMail), and build out the AI observability layer.

## What already exists
- `ExtractBookingRequest` service: regex-based extraction of event date, headcount, and budget; sets `missing_fields`, `review_reasons`, and `status`
- `AiRun` model for persisting AI run metadata (not yet populated)
- `BookingRequest` state machine with `pending`, `reviewing`, `approved`, `rejected`

## Remaining scope
- Integrate an LLM call for extraction (replace or augment regex with structured output)
- Validate LLM structured output before applying to `BookingRequest`
- Implement decisioning: follow-up needed vs ready for review vs human handoff
- Apply confidence-based routing (low confidence → force reviewing state)
- Persist `AiRun` rows with prompt version, model version, raw response, and latency
- Make every AI run inspectable via the ops dashboard

## Requirements
- AI assists decisions but does not own canonical state transitions
- Structured output must be validated before use
- Every AI run must be auditable (prompt version, model version, raw response)
- The extraction pipeline must remain provider-agnostic so it works the same for IMAP and AgentMail inputs

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] LLM extraction produces structured fields with a confidence indicator
- [ ] Decisioning routes messages to follow-up, review, or ready states
- [ ] Low-confidence or ambiguous outputs force human review
- [ ] `AiRun` rows are persisted with full metadata for every extraction and decisioning call
- [ ] Specs cover parse failure, low confidence, and happy path cases
- [ ] Implementation follows TDD

## Outcome
Toaster gains an inspectable LLM-assistance layer that improves extraction accuracy and adds decisioning on top of the existing regex foundation across IMAP and AgentMail.

title: Booking request reconciliation and workflow transitions
description:
## Summary
Turn persisted thread and message data into deterministic booking-request updates, workflow transitions, tasks, and event logs.

## Scope
- Reconcile thread and message data into `BookingRequest`.
- Compute missing qualification fields.
- Implement explicit state-transition rules.
- Create follow-up, review, and send tasks as needed.
- Record every important action in `EventLog`.

## Requirements
- `BookingRequest` remains the source of truth.
- State changes must be explicit and validated.
- `EventLog` must be append-only and complete enough for debugging and replay.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] New or updated inbound thread data can create or update a `BookingRequest`.
- [ ] Missing fields are computed in a stable schema.
- [ ] Invalid state transitions are rejected.
- [ ] Valid transitions produce `EventLog` rows.
- [ ] Human-review cases create tasks instead of silently guessing.
- [ ] Service specs cover state transitions and logging side effects.
- [ ] Implementation follows TDD.

## Outcome
Toaster gains the deterministic workflow layer between persisted communication data and operator-facing booking-request state.

title: Gmail webhook connector on provider-agnostic inbox pipeline
description:

Toaster is using IMAP for email whenever possible, and the AgentMail API when working with AgentMail. AgentMail is mainly for testing and transactional system emails, so Gmail push/webhook ingestion is not the right next step.

## Current direction

title: Build Gmail OAuth connection management and watch lifecycle

title: Implement core schema and tenant-safe Rails models with TDD

title: Scaffold project foundation: Rails API, Next.js app, Neon config, and test harness
description:
## Summary
Set up the baseline project structure for the booking assistant using a Rails API backend and Next.js frontend, with Neon Postgres configuration and a strict TDD workflow.

## Scope
- Create backend/ Rails API app
- Create frontend/ Next.js app
- Configure shared repo structure and README basics
- Configure Neon pooled connection for app/workers
- Configure direct connection for migrations/schema tasks
- Add baseline test tooling for Rails and frontend
- Add CI skeleton for backend/frontend test runs

## Requirements
- Backend and frontend can boot locally
- Rails test stack is ready before application logic work begins
- Next.js test setup is present even if minimal
- Connection strategy is documented clearly

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `backend/` and `frontend/` exist with clean initial scaffolds
- [ ] Rails is configured as API-first
- [ ] Neon pooled vs direct connection strategy is implemented and documented
- [ ] RSpec/FactoryBot (or chosen Rails test stack) is installed and runnable
- [ ] Frontend test runner is installed and runnable
- [ ] CI runs at least the baseline backend and frontend tests
- [ ] README explains how to boot both apps locally
- [ ] Work is done in TDD style: test setup exists before feature code is added

## Notes
Keep this issue strictly about foundation. Do not start Gmail or workflow logic here.

title: Remember-me / persistent login for app users
description: Follow-up to session auth (toaster-1777922323859-22). Initial rollout uses default Rails session cookie (browser session). Add optional long-lived sign-in (remember me checkbox, signed cookie, or refresh token) with clear security review and logout semantics.

value: Code should be self-documenting and easy for a human to read: prefer clear names and structure; use comments for non-obvious why (domain rules, invariants, security/perf tradeoffs), not to restate what the next line does.
